plainMeaning: Until I finish, I keep my body still, my mouth silent, and my mind in meditation — letting go of all concern for my body.
recitation: tāva kāyaṁ ṭhāṇeṇaṁ, moṇeṇaṁ, jhāṇeṇaṁ, appāṇaṁ vosirāmi

wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. tāvat ('so long, until then'); the answering correlative of jāva (yāvat) in the preceding verse.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the jāva…tāva bracket opened in verse 4: 'as long as (jāva) I have not concluded… until then (tāva)…'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. kāya ('body'); accusative singular kāyaṁ — the direct object of vosirāmi.
Skt. sthāna ('standing, posture, station'); instrumental singular — 'by means of fixed stance.'
wordByWord.notes: First of the three instrumental means; here it stills the body (kāya-yoga).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. mauna ('silence'); instrumental singular — 'by means of silence.'
Skt. dhyāna ('meditation, concentration'); instrumental singular — 'by means of meditation.'
wordByWord.notes: Stills the mind (mano-yoga); completes the body–speech–mind triad.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ātmānam ('oneself'); Prakrit reflexive appāṇa, accusative appāṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Reinforces kāyaṁ: 'I, of my own accord, set the body aside.' Read by some as 'I abandon (attachment to) my very self/body'; the renunciation is of bodily self-identification, not of the soul.

wordByWord.gloss: I abandon, I give up, I let go
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. vyutsṛjāmi (vi-ut-√sṛj, 'to release, cast off completely'); Prakrit vosirai, first person singular present.
wordByWord.notes: The operative renunciation-verb that governs the whole sentence; the same verb seals many Jain confessional and renunciatory formulae.

literalTranslation: Until then I abandon the body — by posture, by silence, by meditation — I let go of my own self.
idiomaticTranslation: Until that moment I set my body aside: holding it still in posture, keeping the mouth silent, fixing the mind in meditation, I release all hold upon myself.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse supplies the 'tāva' (until-then) that answers the 'jāva' (as-long-as) of the previous line, closing the single vow-sentence into a complete temporal bracket: the kāyotsarga stands in force from this declaration until it is released by the Namaskāra. The grammatical centre is the lone first-person verb vosirāmi ('I abandon') — every other word feeds it. Its object is doubled for emphasis: kāyaṁ ('the body') and appāṇaṁ ('my own self'), so that the reciter renounces not the soul but the reflexive clinging that mistakes the body for the self. The three instrumentals ṭhāṇeṇaṁ–moṇeṇaṁ–jhāṇeṇaṁ are the verse's heart: they map exactly onto the three yogas — body, speech, and mind. Posture (sthāna) silences bodily activity, silence (mauna) silences speech, meditation (dhyāna) silences the wandering mind. Only when all three channels of karmic influx are stilled does 'abandoning the body' become real rather than postural. Note the precision: the reciter does not claim to destroy the body but to 'set it aside' (vyutsarga) — to withdraw identification and attention from it for the measured duration. Thus the sutra's earlier list of permitted reflexes (breathing, the subtle saṁcāla) and this closing vow fit together perfectly: the body may still twitch and breathe, but the practitioner has formally released ownership of it. The verse therefore converts the abstract noun kāyotsarga into a first-person act, spoken in the present tense, here and now.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The triad ṭhāṇa–moṇa–jhāṇa is the liturgical face of the doctrine that all karmic bondage flows through the three yogas (mana, vacana, kāya); stilling them is the gupti (restraint) that arrests fresh influx. Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.19 defines vyutsarga (here, kāyotsarga) precisely as the relinquishing of body, speech, and mind in meditation — making this verse a recited enactment of that definition. The verb vosirāmi expresses vyutsarga-tapas, an internal austerity (abhyantara-tapa): renunciation not of an object but of attachment, the very mechanism of saṁvara and nirjarā.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When you reach this line, let it become a checklist for the stillness about to begin: feet planted (ṭhāṇa), lips sealed (moṇa), attention gathered onto a single object such as the Logassa count (jhāṇa). Saying vosirāmi in the present tense is the cue to release — to stop monitoring posture and itches and simply let the body be, treating its small reflexes (already permitted earlier) as none of your concern until the closing Navkar.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.19 (vyutsarga / kāyotsarga as relinquishing body, speech, mind)
Uttarādhyayana-sūtra 29 (threefold guarding of body, speech, mind in penance)
crossReferences.source: Jacobi, Sacred Books of the East vol. 45

crossReferences.text: Tassa Uttari (paired sutra: the resolve that this kāyotsarga be done for purification)
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha ed.)

sources.title: Kayotsarga
sources.consultedFor: kāyotsarga as abandonment of the body and its standing/sitting meditative form

sources.title: Pratikramana
sources.consultedFor: placement of Kāyotsarga among the six Āvaśyakas and the body–speech–action framework

sources.title: Tattvartha Sutra
sources.consultedFor: doctrinal definition of vyutsarga/kāyotsarga (9.19) as relinquishing body, speech, and mind

notes: tāva is the correlative 'until then' (Skt. tāvat), NOT 'your' — a common misreading; it answers jāva in verse 4, so vv. 4–5 form one sentence. ṭhāṇa here is sthāna ('posture/standing'), not 'renunciation.' The double object kāyaṁ…appāṇaṁ is read two ways across translators: (a) 'I abandon the body, [namely] my own self,' or (b) appāṇaṁ as emphatic reflexive 'I myself abandon the body'; both yield the same sense — withdrawal of identification with the body, not annihilation of the soul. Wording preserved verbatim per the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book; orthography (anusvāras, ṇ-forms) not normalized.
